data.title: Where Was This Knight Before?
data.statement:
You get two consecutive pictures of a game board.  The board is made of 64 cells arranged in 8 lines of 8 columns. A single piece was moved: 
 * either to a free cell (simple move), 
 * or to a cell occupied by another piece, in which case the target is removed from the board (move with capture). 

Some AI already converted the pictures to ASCII art. Each piece is identified with a letter (upper case for one player,  lower case for the other). You just have to write down the move with its coordinates.

testCases.testIn:
RNBQKP
RNBQKBNR
PPPPPPPP
........
........
........
........
pppppppp
rnbqkbnr
RNBQKBNR
PPPPPPPP
........
........
........
....p...
pppp.ppp
rnbqkbnr

data.constraints:
Only one piece is moved and at most one piece can be captured.  
Caution: due to shadows on the picture, the same free cell may look slightly differently in the two pictures (characters not used) .
data.stubGenerator:
read pieces:string(256)
loop 8 read sline:string(8)
loop 8 read eline:string(8)
write answer
data.inputDescription:
<<Line 1 :>> String of letters used for the upper-case [[pieces]].  
<<Line 2-9 :>> The board before the move: 8 lines, each a string of 8 printable ascii chars.  The chess pieces are one of the letters of line 1, in upper-case (white) or in lower-case (black)
<<Line 10-17 :>> The board after the move.
Any printable ascii character on the board that is not a valid piece is an empty cell.
data.outputDescription:
Coordinates of the initial and final cell of the piece that was move. 
Each coordinate is made of one lowercase letter (from 'a' to 'h' for the horizontal position on the board) and one digit (from '1' to '8' for the vertical position), the top left cell being  "a8".  
The coordinates shall be separated by '-' for a simple move, or 'x' for a move with capture.  

Examples:  
`e2-e4`
`e4xf5`

lastVersion.statementHTML:
Goal

You get two consecutive pictures of a game board. The board is made of 64 cells arranged in 8 lines of 8 columns. A single piece was moved:
* either to a free cell (simple move),
* or to a cell occupied by another piece, in which case the target is removed from the board (move with capture).

Some AI already converted the pictures to ASCII art. Each piece is identified with a letter (upper case for one player, lower case for the other). You just have to write down the move with its coordinates.
Input
Line 1 : String of letters used for the upper-case pieces.
Line 2-9 : The board before the move: 8 lines, each a string of 8 printable ascii chars. The chess pieces are one of the letters of line 1, in upper-case (white) or in lower-case (black)
Line 10-17 : The board after the move.
Any printable ascii character on the board that is not a valid piece is an empty cell.
Output
Coordinates of the initial and final cell of the piece that was move.
Each coordinate is made of one lowercase letter (from 'a' to 'h' for the horizontal position on the board) and one digit (from '1' to '8' for the vertical position), the top left cell being "a8".
The coordinates shall be separated by '-' for a simple move, or 'x' for a move with capture.

Examples: 
e2-e4

e4xf5
Constraints
Only one piece is moved and at most one piece can be captured.
Caution: due to shadows on the picture, the same free cell may look slightly differently in the two pictures (characters not used) .
Example
Input
RNBQKP
RNBQKBNR
PPPPPPPP
........
........
........
........
pppppppp
rnbqkbnr
RNBQKBNR
PPPPPPPP
........
........
........
....p...
pppp.ppp
rnbqkbnr
Output
e2-e3
